_schema_notes: Per-state source of truth for open-records / open-meetings / finance-transparency law.
Observers and records-request PDF generators read from this file instead
of hardcoding state-specific statute citations. Add a new state by adding
a top-level key with the same shape; the rest of the pipeline works without
code changes.
open_records_act = the law governing public access to government records
(response deadlines, fee rules, denial procedures).
open_meetings_act = the law governing what must be public about meetings
themselves (minutes, agendas, attendance).
campaign_finance_act = the law governing candidate disclosure filings.
finding_categories = the specific statute each TownWatch finding cites.
SCALE MODEL (added 2026-06): the catalog is keyed per (state x requirement),
NOT per jurisdiction. One verified state block governs every jurisdiction of
every body type in that state, so the human-review unit is ~50 states, not
thousands of towns. Re-verify on legislative-session cadence and diff.
Each finding_category carries two applicability dimensions so ONE statute
serves multiple body types (city council, county commission, board of
education, ...) without duplication:
applies_to_body_types: list of concrete body_types OR class tokens
resolved via body_type_classes below. A requirement attaches to a
CLASS, so a newly-onboarded body type inherits it automatically.
disclosure_mode: how the obligation is satisfied, which decides whether
its absence is an auditable gap:
proactive_post  -> the agency must publish it (website/posting). We
scrape for it; absence => a finding.
on_request      -> public only on request. Absence from a website is
NOT a violation; we surface the records-request
path, not a gap finding.
state_published -> published by the STATE (DOAA/GaDOE/GOSA). We link
to the state source; never a jurisdiction finding.
Only proactive_post categories drive gap findings; on_request / state_published
categories render as links/context. NOTE: public comment at meetings is set by
each board's own policy, not statute, so it is NOT a finding_category — it
feeds the live-forum config (sign-up window), not the audit.
_body_type_classes_doc: body_type_classes expands a class token in applies_to_body_types to concrete body_types. Per-state because government structure varies by state.

open_records_act.title: Georgia Open Records Act
open_records_act.fee_search_note: Search/retrieval/redaction capped at the prorated hourly salary of the lowest-paid full-time employee with the necessary skill; first quarter-hour free (OCGA § 50-18-71(c)(1)).
open_records_act.records_officer_note: Designating an open-records officer is optional; if the agency does, requests must be made in writing to that officer before the 3-business-day clock starts, and the designation must be posted on the agency's website and noticed to the legal organ.

minutes_missing.statute_text: The regular minutes of a meeting shall be promptly recorded and shall be open to public inspection once approved as official by the agency or its committee, but in no case later than immediately following the agency's next regular meeting.

agenda_missing.statute_text: The agenda of all matters expected to come before a public agency shall be available upon request and shall be posted at the meeting site as far in advance as reasonably possible, at a minimum during the two-week period immediately prior to the meeting.

campaign_finance_missing.statute_text: Candidates for elective office shall file Campaign Contribution Disclosure Reports at the deadlines specified by the Act, including pre-election, post-election, and semi-annual reports.

member_roster_missing.statute_text: All public records shall be open for personal inspection and copying by any person at a reasonable time and place. The membership of a public agency — including elected and appointed boards — is a public record.

meeting_notice_too_short.statute_text: Every public agency shall post notice of the time, place, and dates of regular meetings at least one week in advance in a conspicuous place and on its website. Special-called meetings require 24 hours' notice to the legal organ; emergency meetings require notice as soon as reasonable. Notice substantially shorter than the prevailing transparency norm, even when lawful, undermines public participation.

budget_summary_missing.statute_text: Each local board of education shall advertise a summary of its proposed budget and hold at least two public meetings/hearings before final adoption, and shall publish the adopted (and any amended) budget. School-specific budget-transparency requirement, distinct from the general local-government budget act.

millage_advertisement_missing.statute_text: A levying or recommending authority (including a school system) shall compute and advertise the rollback millage rate; if the proposed millage rate exceeds the rollback rate, it shall advertise the increase and hold advertised public hearings before adoption (Taxpayer Bill of Rights). Applies to county, municipal, and school district levies alike.
millage_advertisement_missing.notes: HB 581 (2024) does NOT repeal or amend this duty; it only reduces the rollback rate by the FLOST millage equivalent. Both the advertisement and three-hearing requirements remain fully in force (verified 2026-06-04 against the DOR April-2025 compliance guide).
millage_advertisement_missing.observer_note: CATALOG REFERENCE ONLY — no observer. The verifiable signal for this duty is the newspaper advertisement + rollback-rate computation, neither observable from our agenda-platform sources, so asserting a gap would be a guess. A millage adoption-trail observer (parallel to budget) needs its own category distinct from this advertisement duty; revisit with finance ingestion.

tax_digest_advertisement_missing.statute_text: Each levying authority shall publish a Current Tax Digest and Five-Year History of Levy in a newspaper of general circulation and post it on its website (if any) at least one week before establishing the annual millage rate. Always required each cycle, distinct from the § 48-5-32.1 increase advertisement. HB 581 (2024) does not amend this duty.

budget_process_missing.statute_text: Each unit of general-purpose local government shall place its proposed budget in a public location for inspection, publish a notice of the budget's availability and a notice of the public hearing in a newspaper of general circulation, and hold a public hearing at least one week before adopting the budget by ordinance or resolution. Distinct from the school budget act (§ 20-2-167.1). Enables public voice before the spending decision.
budget_process_missing.observer_note: observe_budget_adoption_overdue asserts ONLY the verifiable absence: a body that demonstrably adopted an annual budget every prior year (>=2 adoption years in our data) and has none in the current cycle despite continued meetings WITH published minutes. The newspaper-advertisement and hearing-notice-timing elements of this statute are NOT observable from our sources and are never asserted.

service_delivery_strategy.statute_text: Each county and its municipalities must adopt a Service Delivery Strategy, filed with and verified by the Department of Community Affairs; a current verified SDS is a precondition for state funding, permits, grants, and loans. State-published: DCA hosts the verified document, so link to the DCA portal rather than flag a jurisdiction gap.

compensation_increase_advertisement_miss.statute_text: A county governing authority shall publish notice of intent to increase its own salary, compensation, or expenses, and the fiscal impact, in the county legal organ once a week for three consecutive weeks immediately preceding the meeting at which the action is taken.

public_works_advertisement_missing.statute_text: Before awarding a public-works construction contract (except those exempt under § 36-91-22 — e.g. under $100,000, emergencies, sole-source), a county or municipality shall post the opportunity conspicuously in the governing authority's office and advertise it in the county legal organ (at least twice, the first at least four weeks before bid opening) or continuously for at least four weeks on its website or the Georgia Procurement Registry.

annual_audit.statute_text: Local governments and school systems must obtain an annual independent audit. School-system audits are filed with and published by the Georgia Department of Audits & Accounts (DOAA School System Financial Dashboard). State-published: link to the state source rather than flag a jurisdiction gap.

state_financial_report.statute_text: GaDOE publishes per-district revenue and expenditure data (Georgia Insights) and GOSA publishes the Financial Efficiency Star Rating. State-published transparency artifacts for school systems; render as links. (CCRPI publisher attribution intentionally omitted — that specific claim did not survive verification.)

_provenance.phase: Tier 1 / Phase A — statute inventory (FOIA core + finance layer COMPLETE)
_provenance.verification: Adversarial 3-of-3 (independent agents prompted to refute; all upheld) on: requester-residency posture, § 30-4-30(C) deadlines, § 30-4-80 agenda/notice, § 30-4-90 minutes, centralized campaign-finance filing (FOIA core); AND § 6-1-80 budget/millage hearing notice, § 6-1-320 millage cap, § 6-1-50 RFA report, § 4-9-150 county audit, § 5-7-240 municipal audit, SC DOE/RFA school finance (finance layer). Two finance corrections encoded from the refuters: municipal audits (§ 5-7-240) are public-inspection only (NOT state-collected, unlike county § 4-9-150 → State Treasurer); § 6-1-50 is gated on 'receiving state aid'. Corroborated by SC Press Association + RCFP + MASC.
_provenance.scope_note: FOIA core (open records + open meetings) + campaign-finance pointer + finance-transparency layer are ALL COMPLETE and verified (2026-06-13). Finance categories: budget_hearing_notice_missing (§ 6-1-80, +§ 6-1-320 cap), county_annual_audit (§ 4-9-150, state-collected), municipal_annual_audit (§ 5-7-240, public-inspection), state_financial_report (§ 6-1-50 RFA), school_financial_report (SC DOE/RFA). Deliberate EXCLUSIONS (confirmed absent 3/3 — do not add): no GA-style rollback-rate advertisement (no § 48-5-32.1 analog), no uniform local public-works-bid advertisement (Title 11 Ch.35 binds STATE bodies; subdivisions self-ordinance), no county council self-compensation triple-publication (§ 4-9-100 sets pay by ordinance). No observer is activated — no finance ingestion exists (STATE_PLAYBOOK Phase-A rule 4); these are catalog/state-published entries until finance ingestion lands.

open_records_act.title: South Carolina Freedom of Information Act
open_records_act.requester_residency_note: Access runs to 'A person' — no citizenship/residency limit, so records requests use the DIRECT-SEND lane (no citizen-proxy needed, unlike VA/TN/AL/AR). NOTE the asymmetry: standing to SUE to enforce FOIA is limited to 'a citizen of the State' (§ 30-4-100), so an ORR non-response escalation to litigation needs a SC-citizen requester of record. Confirmed by SC Press Association ('Does South Carolina's FOIA end at the border?').
open_records_act.response_determination_note: Public body must notify the requester of its determination to comply within 10 business days (excepting Saturdays, Sundays, and legal public holidays); 20 business days if the record is more than 24 months old at the date of request.
open_records_act.production_note: After the determination, records must be furnished no later than 30 calendar days from the date the deposit is received (35 calendar days for records more than 24 months old). A public body may require a deposit of up to 25% of the reasonably anticipated cost before searching/producing.
open_records_act.fee_note: Fees may not exceed the actual cost of search, retrieval, and redaction. Search/retrieval is capped at the prorated hourly salary of the lowest-paid employee with the skill to fulfill the request; no charge for the first two hours for records produced within those two hours.

open_meetings_act.title: South Carolina Freedom of Information Act (open-meetings provisions)
open_meetings_act.structural_note: SC's open-records and open-meetings law are ONE statute (Title 30, Ch. 4), unlike Georgia's two acts. SC's sharpest, most observable transparency trigger is PRE-meeting (the § 30-4-80 24-hour agenda posting incl. the body's website), whereas Georgia's is POST-meeting (the (e)(2)(A) summary). SC has NO post-meeting summary tier and NO numeric minutes deadline.

campaign_finance_act.title: South Carolina Ethics, Government Accountability, and Campaign Reform Act
campaign_finance_act.citation: S.C. Code § 8-13-1308 (certified campaign reports); § 8-13-365 (mandatory electronic filing); § 8-13-100(2) (supervisory office)
campaign_finance_act.filing_destination: South Carolina State Ethics Commission (centralized; NOT a local clerk)
campaign_finance_act.filing_destination_note: Under § 8-13-100(2) the 'appropriate supervisory office' is the State Ethics Commission for ALL filers except candidates for State Senator/Representative — so county and municipal candidates file with the SEC, not a local clerk. § 8-13-365 mandates Internet-based electronic filing that is publicly accessible and searchable. This is the post-2018 consolidated regime (Act 184 of 2018); pre-reform, local candidates filed with county/municipal clerks.
campaign_finance_act.public_search_note: SEC Public Disclosure & Accountability Reporting portal for candidate campaign-disclosure reports (apps.sc.gov/PublicReporting is the legacy committee/lobbyist portal). Because filings are centralized, candidate non-filing is verifiable at ONE state source for the whole state — a strong centralized audit signal once elections/candidate ingestion exists.

agenda_missing.statute_text: An agenda for regularly scheduled or special meetings must be posted on a bulletin board in a publicly accessible place at the office or meeting place of the public body and on a public website maintained by the body, if any, at least twenty-four hours prior to such meetings.
agenda_missing.observer_note: SC's STRONGEST gap signal and the inverse of Georgia: the duty is PRE-meeting, the deadline is a hard 24 hours, and the statute explicitly names the body's own public website as a posting venue — exactly what our daily inventory cadence observes. A body that maintains a website but posts no agenda within the 24-hour window is a clean, statute-cited finding. This is SC's primary Tier-2 recon dimension (agenda-posting timeliness).

minutes_missing.statute_text: All public bodies shall keep written minutes of all of their public meetings, recording the date/time/place, members present and absent, the substance of all matters proposed, discussed, or decided, and any votes taken. The minutes are public records and shall be available within a reasonable time after the meeting except where inconsistent with Section 30-4-70.
minutes_missing.observer_note: CRITICAL cross-state difference: SC sets NO numeric minutes deadline ('within a reasonable time') and has NO post-meeting written-summary tier — so Georgia's hard-clock minutes-overdue observer (next-regular-meeting trigger) DOES NOT transfer. An SC minutes observer cannot fire on a fixed clock; absence must be judged against the body's own historical cadence (pattern-of-practice), never an asserted statutory deadline. Do NOT port the GA _MINUTES_APPROVAL_GRACE_DAYS logic to SC.

meeting_notice_missing.statute_text: All public bodies must give written public notice of their regular meetings at the beginning of each calendar year, including the dates, times, and places of such meetings. Notice of called, special, or rescheduled meetings must be posted as early as is practicable but not later than twenty-four hours before the meeting.

agenda_amended_without_notice.statute_text: Once an agenda for a regularly scheduled or special meeting is posted, no items may be added to the agenda without an additional twenty-four hours notice to the public. An item upon which final action will be taken, or for which there has been no prior opportunity for public comment, may be added only by a two-thirds vote of the members present and voting and upon a finding by the body that an emergency or an exigent circumstance exists.
agenda_amended_without_notice.observer_note: CATALOG REFERENCE ONLY — no observer. This SC-specific duty (a distinct recon dimension: action taken on items absent from the posted agenda without the 2/3-vote + emergency finding) is observable only by diffing the posted agenda against the actions recorded in the minutes — an ingestion capability we do not yet have. Recorded now so a future agenda-vs-minutes observer cites the statute it was built for; never assert this gap from agenda data alone.

member_roster_missing.statute_text: A person has a right to inspect, copy, or receive an electronic transmission of any public record of a public body. Without limiting this right, the names, position, title, and gross compensation of public officers and employees, and final votes and minutes of public bodies, are declared to be public information. The membership of a public body — its elected and appointed officials — is therefore a public record.

campaign_finance_missing.statute_text: Candidates and committees must file certified campaign disclosure reports (initial, quarterly, and pre-election reports) electronically with the South Carolina State Ethics Commission, which maintains an Internet-based filing system that is publicly accessible and searchable. County and municipal candidates file with the Commission, not a local clerk.
campaign_finance_missing.observer_note: Filings are CENTRALIZED at the State Ethics Commission (§ 8-13-100(2)), so — unlike Georgia's pre-2027 City-Clerk model — this is never a per-jurisdiction website gap. We link to the state portal; a candidate-level non-filing finding is detectable from the single state source once elections/candidate ingestion exists.

budget_hearing_notice_missing.statute_text: A county, municipality, special purpose or public service district, and a school district must advertise the public hearing on its next-fiscal-year budget in at least one South Carolina newspaper of general circulation, not less than fifteen days before the hearing. The notice must state the entity's name; the hearing's time, date, and location; the current-year total revenues and expenditures; the proposed next-year revenue and operating expenditures; the projected percentage change; the current-year millage; and the estimated next-year millage. A general-operating millage increase above the prior year is capped at the average prior-year CPI plus the entity's population growth (§ 6-1-320), exceedable only by a two-thirds vote of the governing body's membership for enumerated circumstances.
budget_hearing_notice_missing.observer_note: CATALOG REFERENCE — no observer yet (no finance ingestion). Verifiable signals: the newspaper advertisement (NOT observable from our agenda sources, same as GA's millage ad) plus the public hearing and the budget adoption itself (observable in minutes/agenda). A future budget observer may assert ONLY the verifiable absence — a body that adopted an annual budget in prior years and has none in the current cycle despite continued meetings — never the ad-timing or hearing-notice elements. SC has NO Georgia-style rollback-rate advertisement (no § 48-5-32.1 analog, confirmed 3/3); § 6-1-80 is the sole budget-related ad, so a 'missing rollback ad' is a non-finding in SC.

county_annual_audit.statute_text: The county council shall provide for an independent annual audit of all financial records and transactions of the county by a certified public accountant or public accountant. A copy of the audit report must be submitted to the State Treasurer no later than January first; if not timely filed, funds the State Treasurer would distribute to the county in the current fiscal year are withheld pending receipt. (2023 Act 71 moved this collection and withholding from the Comptroller General to the State Treasurer.)
county_annual_audit.observer_note: State-collected: county audits are filed with and withholding-enforced by the SC State Treasurer, so absence is never a per-jurisdiction website finding — link to the state source. Contrast municipal_annual_audit (§ 5-7-240), which is public-inspection only.

municipal_annual_audit.statute_text: A municipality with total recurring general-fund revenue of $500,000 or more (for fiscal years beginning after January 1, 2024) shall provide for an independent annual audit of its financial statements by a certified public accountant; smaller municipalities may instead obtain a compilation. The report of the audit or compilation shall be made available for public inspection.
municipal_annual_audit.observer_note: Public-inspection ONLY — § 5-7-240 names no state recipient (confirmed 3/3; unlike the county audit it is NOT filed with the State Treasurer or RFA). Absence from a municipal website is therefore not a violation; surface the records-request path, not a gap finding.

state_financial_report.statute_text: Counties and municipalities receiving revenues from state aid (Aid to Subdivisions) shall submit annually, by March fifteenth, a financial report to the SC Revenue and Fiscal Affairs Office detailing sources of revenue, expenditures by category, indebtedness, and other information; failure to file within thirty days of written notice withholds ten percent of subsequent state-aid payments until filed. RFA is the state publisher of local-government financial data.
state_financial_report.observer_note: State-published: filed with and published by RFA — link to the state source, never a per-jurisdiction gap. Duty is gated on 'receiving state aid' (effectively universal). 'Financial report' is the statutory term; the proper noun 'Local Government Finance Report' is RFA/MASC usage, not statutory.

school_financial_report.statute_label: S.C. Code Title 59 (district financial reporting to SC DOE)
school_financial_report.statute_text: South Carolina school districts report financial data — revenues, expenditures, and audited Statements of Expenditures — upward to the SC Department of Education, which publishes district-level financial data at the state level (ESSA per-pupil expenditures, In$ite financial data, salary schedules, assessed valuation and millage). The SC Revenue and Fiscal Affairs Office additionally runs an Education Funding Dashboard built from districts' reported data.
school_financial_report.observer_note: State-published: per-district financial data is centralized at SC DOE (+ the RFA Education Funding Dashboard) — render as links, never a per-district website finding. The reporting DUTY runs upward to SC DOE; the public-facing publication is state-level. Cite is general (Title 59 / the DOE reporting framework) rather than a single pinned section — hence medium confidence on the statutory pin, though the state publication itself is verified live.

_sc_finance_layer_done._doc: Phase-A finance-transparency statute inventory COMPLETE, verified 2026-06-13 from primary sources (adversarial 3/3). Replaces the prior _sc_finance_layer_todo. SC's analogs were read from SC titles directly — none of Georgia's OCGA citations were ported.
categories_added: budget_hearing_notice_missing (§ 6-1-80 budget + millage hearing notice; § 6-1-320 millage cap) — all levying authorities; proactive_post, catalog-reference observer.
county_annual_audit (§ 4-9-150) — county; state_published (State Treasurer collects, withholding; 2023 Act 71 moved it from the Comptroller General).
municipal_annual_audit (§ 5-7-240) — municipality; on_request (public-inspection only, ≥$500k recurring revenue; NOT state-collected — the key SC vs county distinction).
state_financial_report (§ 6-1-50) — county + municipal; state_published (RFA, by March 15, gated on receiving state aid).
school_financial_report (Title 59 / SC DOE) — board of education; state_published (SC DOE financial-data portal + RFA Education Funding Dashboard); medium confidence on the statutory pin.
deliberate_exclusions: NO rollback-rate newspaper advertisement — SC has no analog to GA OCGA § 48-5-32.1; § 6-1-80 is the sole budget ad. A 'missing rollback ad' is a non-finding (confirmed 3/3).

NO uniform local public-works-bid advertisement — no analog to GA OCGA § 36-91-20. The Consolidated Procurement Code (Title 11 Ch. 35) binds STATE bodies; political subdivisions adopt their own procurement ordinances (§ 11-35-5320), unenforceable as a state mandate (confirmed 3/3).

NO county council self-compensation triple-publication — no analog to GA OCGA § 36-5-24. § 4-9-100 sets council pay by ordinance, effective only at the next general election, with no special advance-publication rule (confirmed 3/3).
_sc_finance_layer_done.residual: No finance observer is activated — no finance ingestion exists yet (STATE_PLAYBOOK Phase-A rule 4). When finance ingestion lands, the Phase-C fiscal-dependency split decides the levying-authority target: for the ~22 fiscally dependent districts a millage/budget finding attaches to County Council, not the board (see research/sc_recon/STRUCTURE.md).
